content: 113. Statement of Armed Police Force (APF) Deputy Inspector General Suresh Kumar Shrestha:

After receiving information via the Armed Police Force Nepal, No. 18 Battalion HQ, Nilbarahi, that the organizers of the September 8 (Bhadra 23) Gen-Z protest had submitted an application to the District Administration Office stating the protest would remain peaceful, an integrated security plan was prepared for the district battalions, and effective mobilization of the Armed Police was arranged. During the protest called by the Gen-Z generation, I was at the Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari, along with the Chief of the Valley Police Office and the Chief of the National Investigation Department, to ensure effective security mobilization of subordinate units and coordination with other security agencies. Based on the decisions of the District Security Committee, the Armed Police Force commanders in all three districts were advised to carry out mobilization in accordance with the APF's operational guidelines while maintaining effective coordination with other security agencies. Information regarding protesters breaching the security perimeter and entering the Federal Parliament building premises, along with other full incident details, was received through the Commandant of APF Nepal No. 30 Battalion, Superintendent of Police Jeevan Kumar K.C. After the situation became uncontrollable and chaotic, a curfew was issued. I received reports via regional communication that the situation had reached the stage of using maximum force as per the principle of use of force, and that some tear gas and blank fires were used by the Armed Police Force. Subsequently, evaluating that the medical teams and ambulances deployed in the field by the APF were insufficient, I coordinated with the Nepal APF Hospital to arrange for an ambulance with a medical team to be deployed to the Parliament building.

Regarding the 23rd, while the curfew order was already in effect from the Kathmandu District Administration Office, I received a report via the regional control that the commanders on duty, exercising the authority granted by the Local Administration Act, had fired a total of 19 rounds of blank fire (10 rounds from INSAS rifles and 9 rounds from .12 bore rifles) as a final resort. I did not receive any direct instructions from the higher office during the process of using force and monitoring the situation.

The orders and instructions were based on prevailing laws, the Armed Police Force Act and Regulations, and the regular training and exercises of subordinate units. Therefore, the decision regarding the use of force was carried out according to the process where decisions can be taken based on individual responsibility and the assessment of the actual situation in the field. When the possibility of bringing the situation under control through minimum force and other alternatives was exhausted, firing shots was an inevitable final resort to control a large and aggressive mob. From the moment I learned of the casualties, my priority was to save the lives of the injured, minimize damage, activate the medical emergency response, and control the situation through restraint and coordination.

On the 24th, I gave necessary instructions to subordinate units for effective security management during potential protests or movements. Preparation was ensured regarding security plans, unit mobilization, the principle of minimum force, emergency medical management, and other security measures. In accordance with the integrated security plan, from 08:00 AM on September 9 (Bhadra 24), Armed Police duty with weapons was mobilized across 27 important locations within the valley, including Singhadurbar, the Parliament building, Baluwatar, and Shital Niwas.

He describes the situation on the 24th as deteriorating rapidly from early morning, with the mob growing large and displaying violent tendencies. Coordination between the administration, Nepal Police, and APF was insufficient, the crowd size increased abnormally in many places, and simultaneous attacks on multiple sensitive points made the division of force and prioritization difficult. As the situation became extremely dangerous, the violent mob set fire to his own vehicle and two trucks used for reinforcements. When the protesters attacked the Valley Police Office, some Nepal Police personnel deployed there left the office for other locations. However, he attempted to keep subordinate officers, Nepal Police, and National Investigation personnel safe, prioritizing their safety over his personal security.

Even on the 24th, in the process of maintaining peace and security, 600 rounds of tear gas shells, 210 rounds of blank fire, and 140 rounds of live fire were discharged by the Armed Police, but no human casualties occurred from these discharges.

Reports were received that during the demonstrations on September 8 and 9 (Bhadra 23 and 24), protesters committed arson and vandalism at the APF Nepal Security Base, Minbhawan, looting SLR- 10 units, SMG- 1 unit, Gas Gun- 4 units, and ammunition used in the 7.62. Additionally, under the APF Nepal No. 9 Pashupatinath Brigade, 1 ambulance, 2 one-ton trucks, 6 Mahindra Scorpios, 2 Riot Control vehicles, 1 Water Cannon, 3 other trucks, and 3 motorcycles were completely destroyed by fire. Furthermore, 141 Armed Police personnel deployed on duty were injured.

According to him, even amidst those events that exceeded riot control capacity, he continued with constant radio command, direct monitoring, fact collection, and situational assessment. He repeatedly issued instructions to his subordinate officers not to give emotional reactions, not to use unauthorized violence or force, not to go beyond the law, and to remain restrained even in uncomfortable situations.

In his statement, he says: 'The role of security agencies was not limited only to riot control: duties such as saving lives, protecting public structures, maintaining the morale of security personnel, and performing restrained legal enforcement in an extremely complex environment had to be fulfilled simultaneously.'

When asked about personal feelings, he says: 'In such a difficult situation, it is not appropriate to express personal opinions or emotional reactions; as an officer, I remained focused on the protection of human life, public safety, legal implementation, and the proper utilization of force and resources.'
114. Statement of Armed Police Senior Superintendent Rajesh Upreti, deployed for the security of Singhadurbar:

On September 9 (Bhadra 24), I was mobilized in a coordinating role for the team deployed for the security of Singhadurbar. With the responsibility of maintaining necessary coordination with the Pashupatinath Brigade Headquarters from the Head Office, I reached Singhadurbar at approximately 8:45 AM; my call sign was 6-7. As per the security plan, the Armed Police Force and Nepal Police were deployed jointly. In the initial stage, an attempt was made for about half an hour outside the main entrance to persuade and stop approximately 4,000–5,000 protesters who had arrived from the Bhadrakali side. However, the psychology of the crowd changed abruptly over time; the seemingly peaceful crowd gradually became aggressive, pushing against us until they reached the wall. We pushed the crowd back using tear gas shells, but as the crowd became even more radical, the decision had to be made to move the team inside through a small side door.

During this process, I deeply realized how complex and risky crowd control can be. Due to the events of the previous day, there was some hesitation visible among the security personnel, and news of vandalism and arson occurring at other locations received via radio sets further increased the mental pressure. I experienced that a crowd becomes even more lethal when it reaches a state where it disregards consequences and even its own life. Provocative statements like 'The security forces won't fire today, let's move forward' were heard from within the crowd, which was making the situation even more sensitive.

No lethal weapons were used or fired by the team under my command that day. Until approximately 3:10 PM, the joint team of the Armed Police Force and Nepal Police attempted to prevent the protesters from entering using gas guns and baton charges, and even those who entered twice were driven out through the same means. However, the situation became extremely grave when some individuals toward the West Gate began pointing SLR weapons toward us. At that moment, we had two choices: fire upon the armed individuals or retreat. I saw the potential for massive human casualties if we fired and, using my own discretion, decided not to fire. There was no condition to seek orders for that decision.

In the meantime, seeing a female police officer being pulled by the mob, I moved forward to rescue her, after which I was slightly injured by a brick strike to the head from close range. Some Armed Police personnel with me were also slightly injured, but no one sustained serious injuries. The Nepal Army was also present there, and blank fires were even discharged to push the crowd back, but I do not have direct information regarding the Army's internal duties and decision-making processes.

Looking at the nature of the movement, the Gen-Z youth seen initially gradually disappeared, and individuals with the intent to burn Singhadurbar, who had cocked weapons, came to the front. Therefore, I feel there was planned infiltration in this movement. The Gen-Z youth themselves were saying 'We should not enter Singhadurbar,' but the violent activities seen later do not seem to have happened without prior planning.

The overall sequence of events made me feel that our crowd control capacity, equipment, and preparation remain insufficient. A lack of non-lethal equipment like body cameras, drones, and water cannons was felt, and the command and control system appeared to need further strengthening. I concluded that regular joint exercises, simulation training, and a clear operational SOP between both police organizations are necessary. Additionally, I believe that until clear legal and institutional boundaries are set regarding when the Nepal Police and when the Armed Police Force are to be mobilized, the situation of 'everyone's responsibility is no one's responsibility' may recur.

To prevent such incidents from recurring in the future, I believe this should be viewed not merely as an individual weakness but as a matter requiring systemic reform. I see measures like an Early Warning System, Unified SOP, mechanisms to control rumors spreading via social media, crisis coordination among security agencies, dialogue with youth, and community policing as indispensable. It is my opinion that such tragic incidents can only be prevented in the future if the Commission of Inquiry evaluates the incident impartially, reviews the chain of command, decision-making processes, and potential human rights violations from both sides, and provides actionable recommendations.
145. Statement of Armed Police Force Superintendent Rajesh Lamichhane, Commandant of APF Nepal No. 21 Battalion Dakshinkali, Lalitpur:

I stated that although no formal program for the Gen-Z protest was scheduled in Lalitpur district on September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I was aware that demonstrations could occur in Kathmandu. Accordingly, personnel were deployed to Tinkune in Kathmandu and Balkumari in Lalitpur district. On that day, I was stationed at the Command Post of the District Security Committee, serving as the District Commander for Lalitpur.

According to him, no special activity or large crowds were observed in Lalitpur on September 8 (Bhadra 23). Information was received via communication that the additional personnel deployed to Tinkune had used 64 tear gas shells and fired 3 rounds of 7.62 mm blank fire while stationed near Gate No. 2 of the Federal Parliament Building in New Baneshwor following the issuance of the curfew order in Kathmandu. Following news of human casualties in Kathmandu and keeping in view the sequence of events there on September 8, 2025, a prohibited area was declared throughout Lalitpur district effective from 6:00 PM until further notice, and a curfew order was issued effective from 7:00 AM the following morning until 12:00 midnight.

On September 9 (Bhadra 24), in the process of preventing incidents of vandalism, arson, and looting of government, private, and other properties across the country including Lalitpur district, locations such as the Nepali Congress Central Office, CPN-UML Central Office, Prachanda's residence in Khumaltar, Nakkhu Jail, and the Minister's Quarters became tense. He stated that the deployed Armed Police teams used 139 tear gas shells, 53 units of 7.62 mm blank rounds, and 33 rounds of blank fire to perform crowd control as per the principle of minimum use of force. On that day, no protesters were injured or killed by the use of weapons in Lalitpur.

During the protest on that day, 10 Armed Police personnel were injured, and a riot control vehicle deployed in the Minister's Quarters area, one Scorpio vehicle, and 2 private motorcycles belonging to the Armed Police were destroyed by fire. Despite the challenges arising from the intensity of the incident,
he mentioned that necessary coordination was maintained between the Nepal Police, Armed Police, and the Nepal Army, and that the Nepal Army provided significant assistance in the Nakkhu Jail area.

He stated that due to the presence of unidentified groups carrying rods and domestic weapons in the movement, infiltration could be suspected, though he does not possess proof. Regarding the relationship between complaints of excessive force and deaths, he says: 'Final force should only be used in inevitable circumstances; therefore, the investigation should be conducted in a balanced manner based on facts and evidence, otherwise, it may negatively impact the morale of security personnel deployed in the field.'

Finally, noting that the root cause of the events of the 23rd and 24th was the social condition arising from dissatisfaction regarding public aspirations, good governance, service delivery, and employment, he suggests that to prevent such incidents in the future, improvements in citizen services, development of technology-based systems, clear demarcation of authority among security mechanisms, adequate availability of non-lethal weapons, and policy-level improvements in crowd management capacity for places like the capital are necessary.
116. Statement of Armed Police Force (APF) Superintendent Roshan Ghimire, Commandant of APF Nepal No. 18 Battalion HQ, Nilbarahi, Kathmandu:

On September 7, 2025 (Bhadra 22), through a District Security Committee meeting, the National Investigation Department provided information that approximately 1,500–2,500 protesters, including students, were expected to gather for the Gen-Z demonstration on September 8 (Bhadra 23) and conduct the program peacefully. Based on this, an integrated security plan was prepared with rehearsals, briefings, and coordination. I was stationed at the District Command Post. Following the security plan received from the APF Nepal No. 9 Pashupatinath Brigade Headquarters, Kathmandu, Armed Police personnel were deployed to the District Administration Office Kathmandu mode, Bhadrakali, and the T.U. Gate. The Gen-Z demonstration remained peaceful at those locations without any clashes occurring.

On September 8 (Bhadra 23), while at the District Command Post, information received via radio set indicated that although the gathering starting from Maitighar Mandala was initially peaceful, the aggressiveness of the crowd increased after entering the Baneshwor area. Consequently, the curfew order issued at 12:30 PM was defied by a large, anarchic mob. Field commanders repeatedly used megaphones and warnings, and while operating according to the principle of minimum use of force, blank fires were discharged near the Parliament building. During that demonstration, 103 Armed Police personnel were injured, and protesters also vandalized an APF ambulance.

Regarding potential events on the 24th, a security plan was prepared by the APF Nepal No. 9 Pashupatinath Brigade Headquarters, Kathmandu. Accordingly, personnel were deployed at the platoon level (35 personnel under an APF Inspector) at 17 different locations under the command of DSPs/Inspectors. As vandalism, arson, and stone-pelting intensified in Kathmandu, additional personnel and equipment were dispatched. On that day, the 24th, the Armed Police attempted to control the mob using 79 tear gas shells, 28 blank rounds, and 19 blank fires. He stated that no information was received regarding anyone being injured by the blank fires. On that day, a constable deployed at the main gate of New Baneshwor was seriously injured and rendered unconscious by a mob attack, and his SLR rifle was looted; 61 Armed Police personnel were injured that day.

According to Ghimire, it appears there was infiltration by various unidentified groups in the circumstances of both days, though he possesses no direct evidence. His understanding is that although the Nepal Army was stationed around the Parliament building, it did not have the expected impact. While expressing regret over the heavy losses despite the efforts of security personnel to protect sensitive structures and public safety amidst dangerous conditions while performing their duties, he submitted suggestions to the Commission. To prevent such incidents in the future, he recommends addressing the shortage of non-lethal equipment, developing a clear coordination structure among security agencies, strengthening technology-based information systems, and providing additional training on crowd behavior psychology.
117. Statement of Armed Police Force (APF) Nepal No. 20 Battalion HQ (Disaster Relief) Sinamangal Commandant and District Security Committee Member, Superintendent of Police Jeevan K.C.:

I was not aware of the Gen-Z youth generation having prior permission for the protest on September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23); however, a meeting of the District Security Committee on September 7 (Bhadra 22) decided to create an integrated security plan involving all four security agencies to arrange security in a coordinated manner. According to that plan and the security plan of APF Nepal No. 9 Pashupatinath Brigade, forces were mobilized from the morning of the 23rd at Maitighar, prohibited areas, and sensitive locations using personnel from Kathmandu's No. 20 and 18 Battalions, No. 24 Company Jamal, No. 21 Battalion Lalitpur, No. 15 Battalion Kavre, No. 22 Battalion Bhaktapur, and the APF Academy Matatirth. Necessary coordination was maintained with the Nepal Police at the district and field levels.

On September 8 (Bhadra 23), I was in overall command at the Command Post located at the District Administration Office, Babarmahal, with the call sign 'Kilo Charlie-1.' There were 638 Armed Police personnel deployed under my command. Forces were stationed at a minimum platoon level at Maitighar Mandala, the Supreme Court, in front of Everest Hotel, the District Administration Office mode, Tinkune, Bhadrakali, and the Bagmati Bridge. Additionally, Field Reserve, Unit Reserve, Water Cannon, and Ambulance units were kept in a state of readiness.

Around 9:30 AM, a peaceful crowd of 300–350 people was seen, but by the time they reached the front of Everest Hotel, the number increased to over 3,000 and gradually became aggressive. Around 11:30 AM, after the crowd reached 20,000–25,000, attempts were made to break the barricades of the prohibited area. After breaking the barricades, the mob attempted to surround the Parliament building and enter with motorcycles. Despite the use of megaphones, persuasion, water cannons, baton charges, and tear gas (AT), it became difficult to bring the crowd under control. After stone-pelting, vandalism, and arson began in the Baneshwor area, tear gas and blank fires had to be used according to the principle of minimum use of force to prevent entry into the Parliament building.

According to the decision of the District Security Committee, a curfew order was issued starting at 12:30 PM. Although information about the curfew was provided via megaphones, the unruly mob continued to defy it. I personally entered the Parliament building from the Command Post for field command and control. The use of water cannons, baton charges, tear gas, and ultimately blank fires was carried out according to the principle of minimum force.

Following the incident, information arrived regarding 17 deaths, hundreds of injured protesters, and a large number of injured personnel from the Nepal Police and Armed Police. I did not have confirmed facts as to whether all deaths and injuries resulted specifically from the use of force. From the APF side, 50 rounds of blank fire from INSAS, 9 rounds from SLR, and 266 tear gas shells were used. Even though some staff became agitated when an ambulance was set on fire, I maintained command and control and instructed them to exercise restraint.

Evaluating the challenges likely to occur on September 9 (Bhadra 24), the curfew was re-issued from 08:30 AM, and approximately 958 personnel were mobilized under my overall command. Additional forces including 3 SSPs and 7 SPs were mobilized from the Head Office. Forces were stationed at more than 17 locations, and Field Reserve, Unit Reserve, Water Cannon, and Ambulance units were kept ready.

However, due to destructive groups infiltrating the crowd, it became difficult to prevent all physical damage with limited resources and manpower. Arson and vandalism occurred at various government offices, constitutional commissions, party offices, VIP residences, and security bases. The Minbhawan security base was completely damaged, and weapons and materials were looted. To take control of the situation in Kathmandu, 407 tear gas shells, 143 blank rounds, and 33 rounds of blank fire from .12 bore rifles were used.

On the 24th, a policy of minimum force was adopted with the aim of preventing further human casualties amidst the anarchy seen in the capital and across the country. However, due to the lack of non-lethal weapons, it was felt that casualties increased when lethal weapons had to be used in certain situations. It appeared that there was planned infiltration by various groups into the protest, although I did not have solid evidence of this in my hands.

In my capacity as the overall commander for the Kathmandu district of the Armed Police Force Nepal, while feeling some shortcomings in not being able to keep the entire situation under control, I see that the field commanders were mobilized according to the principle of minimum force to prevent further human loss under limited resources, manpower, and extreme circumstances. To prevent such incidents in the future, I see a need for training, mobilization policy, non-lethal weapons, logistics, morale boosting, intelligence improvement, clear demarcation with the Nepal Police, and additional Vital Installation Security Units.

I express confidence that the Commission of Inquiry will conduct a factual investigation and reach a just conclusion regarding the use of force, the role of security personnel, available resources, and the circumstances. The events of September 8 and 9 seem linked to growing frustration among the youth, corruption, and weaknesses in service delivery. My understanding is that some elements attempted to destabilize the state by capitalizing on this. I consider it necessary to strengthen the capacity, policy, logistics, and morale of security personnel in the days to come.
118. Statement of Armed Police Force (APF) Nepal No. 20 Battalion HQ (Disaster Relief) Kathmandu Chief, Deputy Superintendent of Police Suman Chalise:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I was deployed as a platoon commander in front of the Everest Hotel in New Baneshwor, as per the security plan prepared by the District Security Committee's decisions and operations branch. [Note: Original copy of statement Page-8, under Serial No. 117 of Schedule-5].
I have received training regarding the Use of Force. Since the initial estimation was that the protest would remain peaceful, the security plan was prepared on that basis.

When I was deployed for duty that day, I received 18 SLR units, 1,800 rounds of ammunition, 9 gas guns, and 180 tear gas shells from APF No. 20 Battalion, and I was on duty with the call sign KC-6. Following the demonstrations, 6 rounds of blank fire and 50 tear gas shells were expended, and I returned the remaining materials to the office. Although the protest was initially peaceful, the crowd grew 10–12 times larger than estimated. My team and I, positioned 100 meters behind the Nepal Police deployment, witnessed the crowd breaking through the barricades. We had estimated the crowd would only arrive from the Bijulibazar side; since there were no barricades on other routes at that location, the sudden entry from all directions made the situation complex, creating a risky environment where the police themselves were caught in the middle of the mob.

After the curfew order was issued at 12:30 PM on September 8, the Nepal Police and Armed Police provided information through joint megaphones, but the crowd did not implement the order. Later, when the mob aggressively pushed the gate at the West Gate of the Parliament building to attempt entry, we initially used megaphones, persuasion, baton charges, and tear gas. However, seeing that thousands of protesters were about to enter a sensitive structure, I took direct permission as a commander from SP Jeevan K.C., who had arrived at the location, and fired 6 blank rounds from a .12 bore rifle. We were instructed from the morning not to use final force (shooting) and to attempt containment as much as possible; therefore, no lethal fire was used except for blank fires. No shots were fired from my team toward protesters outside the Parliament building premises.

Following the use of force, I attempted to rescue and transport the injured from both the police and protester sides to the hospital. However, after the mob set fire to the APF Hospital ambulance, we had to find alternative arrangements. Out of the 103 Armed Police personnel injured that day, 27 were seriously injured, and two (ASI Gita Khatri and Constable Kumar Lama) were immediately rushed to the hospital. I only returned from duty at 11:54 PM (23:54) that night.
